title: Audited Skill-Graph Self-Improvement for Agentic LLMs via Verifiable Rewards, Experience Synthesis, and Continual Memory
comment: 11 pages, 4 figures. Includes a complete runnable reference implementation and audit logging framework
summary:
Reinforcement learning is increasingly used to transform large language models into agentic systems that act over long horizons, invoke tools, and manage memory under partial observability. While recent work has demonstrated performance gains through tool learning, verifiable rewards, and continual training, deployed self-improving agents raise unresolved security and governance challenges: optimization pressure can incentivize reward hacking, behavioral drift is difficult to audit or reproduce, and improvements are often entangled in opaque parameter updates rather than reusable, verifiable artifacts.
  This paper proposes Audited Skill-Graph Self-Improvement (ASG-SI), a framework that treats self-improvement as iterative compilation of an agent into a growing, auditable skill graph. Each candidate improvement is extracted from successful trajectories, normalized into a skill with an explicit interface, and promoted only after passing verifier-backed replay and contract checks. Rewards are decomposed into reconstructible components derived from replayable evidence, enabling independent audit of promotion decisions and learning signals. ASG-SI further integrates experience synthesis for scalable stress testing and continual memory control to preserve long-horizon performance under bounded context.
  We present a complete system architecture, threat model, and security analysis, and provide a fully runnable reference implementation that demonstrates verifier-backed reward construction, skill compilation, audit logging, and measurable improvement under continual task streams. ASG-SI reframes agentic self-improvement as accumulation of verifiable, reusable capabilities, offering a practical path toward reproducible evaluation and operational governance of self-improving AI agents.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Audited Skill-Graph Self-Improvement (ASG-SI), a framework for secure, auditable self-improvement in AI agents by compiling reusable skills into a verifiable graph, addressing issues like reward hacking and opaque parameter updates.
AI.motivation: Current self-improving agents face unresolved security and governance challenges, such as reward hacking, untraceable behavioral drift, and entangled parameter updates. These weaknesses hinder audits, reproducibility, and system trustworthiness.
AI.method: ASG-SI iteratively compiles agents into skill graphs: (1)	extracts improvements from successful trajectories, (2)	normalizes skills with explicit interfaces, (3)	verifies skills via replay checks and contracts, (4)	decomposes rewards into auditable components, and (5)	integrates experience synthesis and memory control for scalability. The framework is grounded in a threat model and security analysis.
AI.result: The authors deliver a complete system architecture with a runnable implementation, demonstrating verifier-backed reward construction, skill compilation, audit logging, and measurable performance improvement under continuous tasks. They validate threat modeling and security guarantees through formal analysis.
AI.conclusion: ASG-SI redefines self-improvement as verifiable capability accumulation, offering a scalable path to govern and evaluate autonomous agents securely. It enables reproducible audits, mitigates reward hacking, and separates improvements into reusable skills, advancing responsible AI development.

title: Secure and Governed API Gateway Architectures for Multi-Cluster Cloud Environments
summary: API gateways serve as critical enforcement points for security, governance, and traffic management in cloud-native systems. As organizations increasingly adopt multi-cluster and hybrid cloud deployments, maintaining consistent policy enforcement, predictable performance, and operational stability across heterogeneous gateway environments becomes challenging. Existing approaches typically manage security, governance, and performance as loosely coupled concerns, leading to configuration drift, delayed policy propagation, and unstable runtime behavior under dynamic workloads. This paper presents a governance-aware, intent-driven architecture for coordinated API gateway management in multi-cluster cloud environments. The proposed approach expresses security, governance, and performance objectives as high-level declarative intents, which are systematically translated into enforceable gateway configurations and continuously validated through policy verification and telemetry-driven feedback. By decoupling intent specification from enforcement while enabling bounded, policy-compliant adaptation, the architecture supports heterogeneous gateway implementations without compromising governance guarantees or service-level objectives. A prototype implementation across multiple Kubernetes clusters demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed design. Experimental results show up to a 42% reduction in policy drift, a 31% improvement in configuration propagation time, and sustained p95 latency overhead below 6% under variable workloads, compared to manual and declarative baseline approaches. These results indicate that governance-aware, intent-driven gateway orchestration provides a scalable and reliable foundation for secure, consistent, and performance-predictable cloud-native platforms.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a governance-aware, intent-driven architecture for managing API gateways in multi-cluster cloud environments, addressing issues like policy drift and inconsistent enforcement. A prototype shows significant improvements in policy consistency and performance.
AI.motivation: Organizations face challenges in maintaining consistent policy enforcement, predictable performance, and stability across heterogeneous API gateway environments in multi-cluster and hybrid cloud deployments. Current approaches lead to configuration drift, delayed policy updates, and unstable runtime behavior.
AI.method: The authors present an intent-driven architecture that translates high-level security, governance, and performance objectives into declarative intents. These intents are systematically converted into enforceable gateway configurations, validated through policy checks and telemetry feedback. The design decouples intent specification from enforcement and allows for policy-compliant adaptations, supporting diverse gateway implementations.
AI.result: Experimental results show a 42% reduction in policy drift, a 31% improvement in configuration propagation time, and a p95 latency overhead of less than 6% under varying workloads, compared to traditional manual and declarative approaches.
AI.conclusion: The proposed intent-driven architecture effectively addresses the challenges of managing API gateways in multi-cluster environments, providing a scalable and reliable solution for secure and performance-predictable cloud-native platforms.

title: SyncGait: Robust Long-Distance Authentication for Drone Delivery via Implicit Gait Behaviors
summary: In recent years, drone delivery, which utilizes unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) for package delivery and pickup, has gradually emerged as a crucial method in logistics. Since delivery drones are expensive and may carry valuable packages, they must maintain a safe distance from individuals until user-drone mutual authentication is confirmed. Despite numerous authentication schemes being developed, existing solutions are limited in authentication distance and lack resilience against sophisticated attacks. To this end, we introduce SyncGait, an implicit gait-based mutual authentication system for drone delivery. SyncGait leverages the user's unique arm swing as he walks toward the drone to achieve mutual authentication without requiring additional hardware or specific authentication actions. We conducted extensive experiments on 14 datasets collected from 31 subjects. The results demonstrate that SyncGait achieves an average accuracy of 99.84\% at a long distance ($>18m$) and exhibits strong resilience against various spoofing attacks, making it a robust, secure, and user-friendly solution in real-world scenarios.

AI.tldr: The paper presents SyncGait, a gait-based mutual authentication system for drone delivery that uses a user's arm swing to authenticate without additional hardware, achieving high accuracy and security over long distances.
AI.motivation: Existing drone delivery systems require secure mutual authentication to prevent unauthorized access to valuable packages. Current solutions have limited authentication range and are vulnerable to sophisticated attacks, necessitating a more secure and practical method.
AI.method: SyncGait employs a user's natural arm swing during walking as a unique gait feature for implicit, hardware-free mutual authentication. The system does not require specific user actions and works over long distances.
AI.result: Extensive testing on 14 datasets from 31 subjects showed SyncGait achieves an average accuracy of 99.84% at distances greater than 18 meters and demonstrates strong resistance to spoofing attacks.
AI.conclusion: SyncGait provides a robust, secure, and user-friendly authentication solution for drone delivery, effectively addressing the limitations of existing methods with its hardware-free design and high performance.

title: Prompt-Induced Over-Generation as Denial-of-Service: A Black-Box Attack-Side Benchmark
summary: Large language models (LLMs) can be driven into over-generation, emitting thousands of tokens before producing an end-of-sequence (EOS) token. This degrades answer quality, inflates latency and cost, and can be weaponized as a denial-of-service (DoS) attack. Recent work has begun to study DoS-style prompt attacks, but typically focuses on a single attack algorithm or assumes white-box access, without an attack-side benchmark that compares prompt-based attackers in a black-box, query-only regime with a known tokenizer. We introduce such a benchmark and study two prompt-only attackers. The first is Evolutionary Over-Generation Prompt Search (EOGen), which searches the token space for prefixes that suppress EOS and induce long continuations. The second is a goal-conditioned reinforcement learning attacker (RL-GOAL) that trains a network to generate prefixes conditioned on a target length. To characterize behavior, we introduce Over-Generation Factor (OGF), the ratio of produced tokens to a model's context window, along with stall and latency summaries. Our evolutionary attacker achieves mean OGF = 1.38 +/- 1.15 and Success@OGF >= 2 of 24.5 percent on Phi-3. RL-GOAL is stronger: across victims it achieves higher mean OGF (up to 2.81 +/- 1.38).

AI.tldr: This paper studies methods for inducing large language models to over-generate tokens by creating prompt attacks that force the model to produce excessive responses, and introduces two attackers: an evolutionary algorithm and a reinforcement learning method, which are evaluated on their ability to cause long continuations and over-generation. The results show that the reinforcement learning attacker is more effective in causing over-generation.
AI.motivation: The motivation is the issue of large language models (LLMs) being driven into over-generation, which negatively impacts response quality, increases latency and cost, and can be weaponized as a denial-of-service (DoS) attack. Current research lacks a comprehensive benchmark for comparing different black-box, query-only prompt-side attackers against a known tokenizer.
AI.method: The paper introduces two prompt-based attackers: Evolutionary Over-Generation Prompt Search (EOGen), which uses an evolutionary algorithm to search token space for prefixes that suppress EOS and induce long continuations, and a goal-conditioned reinforcement learning attacker (RL-GOAL), which trains a network to generate prefixes under a target length condition. The performance of these attackers is evaluated using metrics such as the Over-Generation Factor (OGF), which measures the ratio of produced tokens to the model's context window, alongside stall and latency summaries.
AI.result: The evolutionary attacker (EOGen) achieved a mean OGF of 1.38 ± 1.15 and a Success@OGF ≥ 2 of 24.5 percent on Phi-3. The RL-GOAL attacker outperformed these results, achieving a higher mean OGF of up to 2.81 ± 1.38 across different victim models.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that the introduced benchmark allows for a comparison of prompt-based attackers in a black-box, query-only regime. The reinforcement learning attacker (RL-GOAL) demonstrated superior performance in inducing over-generation, achieving higher Over-Generation Factors compared to the evolutionary algorithm. This highlights the potential for more effective DoS-style attacks on LLMs, emphasizing the importance of understanding and mitigating such vulnerabilities.

title: AgenticTCAD: A LLM-based Multi-Agent Framework for Automated TCAD Code Generation and Device Optimization
summary: With the continued scaling of advanced technology nodes, the design-technology co-optimization (DTCO) paradigm has become increasingly critical, rendering efficient device design and optimization essential. In the domain of TCAD simulation, however, the scarcity of open-source resources hinders language models from generating valid TCAD code. To overcome this limitation, we construct an open-source TCAD dataset curated by experts and fine-tune a domain-specific model for TCAD code generation. Building on this foundation, we propose AgenticTCAD, a natural language - driven multi-agent framework that enables end-to-end automated device design and optimization. Validation on a 2 nm nanosheet FET (NS-FET) design shows that AgenticTCAD achieves the International Roadmap for Devices and Systems (IRDS)-2024 device specifications within 4.2 hours, whereas human experts required 7.1 days with commercial tools.

AI.tldr: The paper presents AgenticTCAD, an open-source, natural language-driven framework for automated TCAD device design and optimization, demonstrating its efficiency in meeting IRDS-2024 specifications for a 2 nm NS-FET significantly faster than expert-led manual methods.
AI.motivation: The scarcity of open-source TCAD resources limits the ability of language models to generate valid TCAD code, hindering automated design and optimization as DTCO becomes increasingly critical with advanced technology nodes.
AI.method: The authors create an expert-curated open-source TCAD dataset and fine-tune a domain-specific language model for TCAD code generation. They then develop AgenticTCAD, a multi-agent framework that uses natural language inputs to automate the entire device design and optimization process.
AI.result: AgenticTCAD achieved IRDS-2024 device specifications for a 2 nm nanosheet FET in 4.2 hours, compared to 7.1 days required by human experts using commercial tools.
AI.conclusion: AgenticTCAD offers a significant advancement in automated TCAD simulation by combining an open-source dataset with a domain-adapted language model within a multi-agent framework, dramatically accelerating device design while meeting industry standards.

title: Application-Specific Power Side-Channel Attacks and Countermeasures: A Survey
summary: Side-channel attacks try to extract secret information from a system by analyzing different side-channel signatures, such as power consumption, electromagnetic emanation, thermal dissipation, acoustics, time, etc. Power-based side-channel attack is one of the most prominent side-channel attacks in cybersecurity, which rely on data-dependent power variations in a system to extract sensitive information. While there are related surveys, they primarily focus on power side-channel attacks on cryptographic implementations. In recent years, power-side channel attacks have been explored in diverse application domains, including key extraction from cryptographic implementations, reverse engineering of machine learning models, user behavior data exploitation, and instruction-level disassembly. In this paper, we provide a comprehensive survey of power side-channel attacks and their countermeasures in different application domains. Specifically, this survey aims to classify recent power side-channel attacks and provide a comprehensive comparison based on application-specific considerations.

AI.tldr: This paper is a comprehensive survey of power side-channel attacks across various application domains, providing classifications and comparisons of recent attacks and their countermeasures.
AI.motivation: While existing surveys focus on cryptographic implementations, recent power side-channel attacks have expanded into diverse areas like machine learning and user behavior, necessitating a broader review.
AI.method: The authors conduct a literature review to categorize and compare recent power side-channel attacks and their countermeasures based on application-specific contexts.
AI.result: The paper presents a structured classification of attacks across multiple domains and a detailed comparative analysis, highlighting the effectiveness of various countermeasures in different contexts.
AI.conclusion: The survey emphasizes the need for domain-specific defenses against power side-channel attacks, suggesting that understanding application-specific requirements is crucial for developing effective countermeasures.

title: Hybrid-Code: A Privacy-Preserving, Redundant Multi-Agent Framework for Reliable Local Clinical Coding
summary: Clinical coding automation using cloud-based Large Language Models (LLMs) poses privacy risks and latency bottlenecks, rendering them unsuitable for on-premise healthcare deployment. We introduce Hybrid-Code, a hybrid neuro-symbolic multi-agent framework for local clinical coding that ensures production reliability through redundancy and verification. Our system comprises two agents: a Coder that attempts language model-based semantic reasoning using BioMistral-7B but falls back to deterministic keyword matching when model output is unreliable, ensuring pipeline completion; and an Auditor that verifies codes against a 257-code knowledge base and clinical evidence. Evaluating on 1,000 MIMIC-III discharge summaries, we demonstrate no hallucinated codes among accepted outputs within the knowledge base, 24.47% verification rate, and 34.11% coverage (95% CI: 31.2%--37.0%) with 86%+ language model utilization. The Auditor filtered invalid format codes and provided evidence-based quality control (75.53% rejection rate) while ensuring no patient data leaves the hospital firewall. The hybrid architecture -- combining language model semantic understanding (when successful), deterministic fallback (when the model fails), and symbolic verification (always active) -- ensures both reliability and privacy preservation, addressing critical barriers to AI adoption in healthcare. Our key finding is that reliability through redundancy is more valuable than pure model performance in production healthcare systems, where system failures are unacceptable.

AI.tldr: Hybrid-Code is a hybrid neuro-symbolic multi-agent framework for local clinical coding that ensures reliability through redundancy and verification, addressing privacy and latency issues in healthcare AI deployment.
AI.motivation: Cloud-based LLMs for clinical coding have privacy risks and latency issues, making them unsuitable for on-premise healthcare use.
AI.method: The system uses two agents: a Coder that combines semantic reasoning with BioMistral-7B and deterministic keyword matching, and an Auditor that verifies codes against a knowledge base and clinical evidence. The hybrid approach includes semantic understanding, deterministic fallback, and symbolic verification.
AI.result: On 1,000 MIMIC-III discharge summaries, Hybrid-Code achieved no hallucinated codes within the knowledge base, a 24.47% verification rate, 34.11% coverage (95% CI: 31.2%--37.0%), 86%+ language model utilization, a 75.53% rejection rate for invalid codes, and ensured data privacy by keeping patient data on-premise.
AI.conclusion: The study shows that reliability through redundancy is more valuable than pure model performance in production healthcare systems, as system failures are unacceptable. Hybrid-Code addresses critical barriers to AI adoption in healthcare by ensuring reliability and privacy.

title: VBSF: A Visual-Based Spam Filtering Technique for Obfuscated Emails
summary: Recent spam email techniques exploit visual effects in text messages, such as poisoning text, obfuscating words, and hidden text salting techniques. These effects were able to evade spam detection techniques based on the text. In this paper, we overcome this limitation by introducing a novel visual-based spam detection architecture, denoted as visual-based spam filter (VBSF). The multi-step process mimics the human eye's natural way of processing visual information, automatically rendering incoming emails and capturing their content as it appears on a user screen. Then, two different processing pipelines are applied in parallel. The first pipeline pertains to the perceived textual content, as it includes optical character recognition (OCR) to extract rendered textual content, followed by naive Bayes (NB) and decision tree (DT) content classifiers. The second pipeline focuses on the appearance of the email, as it analyzes and classifies the images of rendered emails through a specific convolutional neural network. Lastly, a meta classifier integrates text- and image-based classifier outputs, exploiting the stacking ensemble learning method. The performance of the proposed VBSF is assessed, showing that it achieves an accuracy of more than 98%, which is higher than the compared existing techniques on the designed dataset.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces VBSF, a visual-based spam detection system that overcomes text-based evasion techniques by combining OCR, text classifiers, and CNN-based image analysis with a meta-classifier, achieving 98.41% accuracy.
AI.motivation: Current text-based spam detection methods fail to identify spam using visual tricks like poisoned text or hidden salting, necessitating a system that processes emails like human vision.
AI.method: VBSF uses parallel pipelines: (1)'perceived text pipeline' with OCR, Naive Bayes, and Decision Tree, and (2)'appearance pipeline' with CNNs for rendered email images, integrated via stacking ensemble learning.
AI.result: VBSF achieved 98.41% accuracy, outperforming existing techniques on the designed dataset.
AI.conclusion: Visual-based analysis (combining perception and appearance) is more effective than text-only methods for combating visually obfuscated spam emails.

title: DEFT: Differentiable Automatic Test Pattern Generation
summary: Modern IC complexity drives test pattern growth, with the majority of patterns targeting a small set of hard-to-detect (HTD) faults. This motivates new ATPG algorithms to improve test effectiveness specifically for HTD faults. This paper presents DEFT (Differentiable Automatic Test Pattern Generation), a new ATPG approach that reformulates the discrete ATPG problem as a continuous optimization task. DEFT introduces a mathematically grounded reparameterization that aligns the expected continuous objective with discrete fault-detection semantics, enabling reliable gradient-based pattern generation. To ensure scalability and stability on deep circuit graphs, DEFT integrates a custom CUDA kernel for efficient forward-backward propagation and applies gradient normalization to mitigate vanishing gradients. Compared to a leading commercial tool on two industrial benchmarks, DEFT improves HTD fault detection by 21.1% and 48.9% on average under the same pattern budget and comparable runtime. DEFT also supports practical ATPG settings such as partial assignment pattern generation, producing patterns with 19.3% fewer 0/1 bits while still detecting 35% more faults. These results indicate DEFT is a promising and effective ATPG engine, offering a valuable complement to existing heuristic.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces DEFT, a novel ATPG algorithm that improves HTD fault detection through continuous optimization and achieves significant improvements over commercial tools in efficiency and fault-detection rates.
AI.motivation: Modern IC complexity leads to test pattern growth, primarily targeting a small set of hard-to-detect (HTD) faults. Traditional ATPG algorithms are less effective for these faults, necessitating new methods to improve test effectiveness.
AI.method: DEFT reformulates the discrete ATPG problem as a continuous optimization task using a reparameterization that aligns with discrete fault-detection semantics. It employs a custom CUDA kernel for scalability and gradient normalization to handle deep circuits, enabling gradient-based pattern generation.
AI.result: DEFT achieves 21.1% and 48.9% improvements in HTD fault detection on two industrial benchmarks compared to a commercial tool, using the same pattern budget and runtime. It also reduces 0/1 bits by 19.3% while detecting 35% more faults in partial assignment settings.
AI.conclusion: DEFT is a promising ATPG approach that effectively addresses limitations of traditional methods for HTD faults, offering superior performance and practical applicability in real-world test pattern generation.

title: Security Without Detection: Economic Denial as a Primitive for Edge and IoT Defense
comment: 8 pages, 2 figures, submitted to 3rd International Conference on Intelligent Digitization of Systems and Services (IDSS2026)
summary: Detection-based security fails against sophisticated attackers using encryption, stealth, and low-rate techniques, particularly in IoT/edge environments where resource constraints preclude ML-based intrusion detection. We present Economic Denial Security (EDS), a detection-independent framework that makes attacks economically infeasible by exploiting a fundamental asymmetry: defenders control their environment while attackers cannot. EDS composes four mechanisms adaptive computational puzzles, decoy-driven interaction entropy, temporal stretching, and bandwidth taxation achieving provably superlinear cost amplification. We formalize EDS as a Stackelberg game, deriving closed-form equilibria for optimal parameter selection (Theorem 1) and proving that mechanism composition yields 2.1x greater costs than the sum of individual mechanisms (Theorem 2). EDS requires < 12KB memory, enabling deployment on ESP32 class microcontrollers. Evaluation on a 20-device heterogeneous IoT testbed across four attack scenarios (n = 30 trials, p < 0.001) demonstrates: 32-560x attack slowdown, 85-520:1 cost asymmetry, 8-62% attack success reduction, < 20ms latency overhead, and close to 0% false positives. Validation against IoT-23 malware (Mirai, Torii, Hajime) shows 88% standalone mitigation; combined with ML-IDS, EDS achieves 94% mitigation versus 67% for IDS alone a 27% improvement. EDS provides detection-independent protection suitable for resource-constrained environments where traditional approaches fail. The ability to detect and mitigate the malware samples tested was enhanced; however, the benefits provided by EDS were realized even without the inclusion of an IDS. Overall, the implementation of EDS serves to shift the economic balance in favor of the defender and provides a viable method to protect IoT and edge systems methodologies.

AI.tldr: EDS is a security framework for IoT/edge systems that makes attacks economically unfeasible by increasing their cost, using four mechanisms to create a cost asymmetry between attackers and defenders.
AI.motivation: Traditional detection-based security fails against sophisticated, stealthy attackers in IoT/edge environments due to their encryption and resource constraints that limit ML-based intrusion detection. This motivates the need for detection-independent security approaches.
AI.method: EDS employs four mechanisms -- adaptive computational puzzles, decoy-driven interaction entropy, temporal stretching, and bandwidth taxation -- to exploit the asymmetry in how defenders and attackers control their environment. It is formalized as a Stackelberg game, providing closed-form equilibria for parameter selection and theoretical guarantees on cost amplification.
AI.result: EDS evaluation on a 20-device IoT testbed showed significant attack slowdowns (32-560x), large cost asymmetry (85-520:1), and reduced attack success (8-62%) with low overhead (<20ms) and no false positives. Against IoT-23 malware, EDS achieved 88% standalone mitigation, and 94% when combined with ML-IDS, a 27% improvement over IDS-only.
AI.conclusion: EDS provides effective detection-independent protection for resource-constrained environments by shifting the economic balance in favor of the defender. It can mitigate attacks without relying on detection, complementing or enhancing traditional detection-based security in IoT and edge systems.

authors: V V S S Prasanth Patnaik
title: State-of-the-art Small Language Coder Model: Mify-Coder
summary: We present Mify-Coder, a 2.5B-parameter code model trained on 4.2T tokens using a compute-optimal strategy built on the Mify-2.5B foundation model. Mify-Coder achieves comparable accuracy and safety while significantly outperforming much larger baseline models on standard coding and function-calling benchmarks, demonstrating that compact models can match frontier-grade models in code generation and agent-driven workflows. Our training pipeline combines high-quality curated sources with synthetic data generated through agentically designed prompts, refined iteratively using enterprise-grade evaluation datasets. LLM-based quality filtering further enhances data density, enabling frugal yet effective training. Through disciplined exploration of CPT-SFT objectives, data mixtures, and sampling dynamics, we deliver frontier-grade code intelligence within a single continuous training trajectory. Empirical evidence shows that principled data and compute discipline allow smaller models to achieve competitive accuracy, efficiency, and safety compliance. Quantized variants of Mify-Coder enable deployment on standard desktop environments without requiring specialized hardware.

AI.tldr: Mify-Coder is a 2.5B-parameter code model trained on 4.2T tokens using compute-optimal strategies, achieving performance comparable to larger models while excelling in coding benchmarks. Its training combines curated/synthetic data with iterative refinement and quantized variants enable desktop deployment.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to demonstrate that compact models can match large-scale models in code generation and agent-driven workflows, addressing the need for efficient yet accurate code AI.
AI.method: Training integrates high-quality curated sources and synthetic data generated via agent-designed prompts, refined with enterprise evaluation datasets. LLM-based filtering and compute-disciplined CPT-SFT approaches optimize data efficiency.
AI.result: Mify-Coder outperforms larger baselines on coding/function-calling benchmarks, achieves safety/accuracy parity, and tenants quantized versions operate on standard desktops without specialized hardware.
AI.conclusion: Compact models with principled data computing strategies can deliver frontier-grade code intelligence, proving efficiency and accuracy are compatible in code generation through disciplined resource management.

title: MeLeMaD: Adaptive Malware Detection via Chunk-wise Feature Selection and Meta-Learning
summary: Confronting the substantial challenges of malware detection in cybersecurity necessitates solutions that are both robust and adaptable to the ever-evolving threat environment. The paper introduces Meta Learning Malware Detection (MeLeMaD), a novel framework leveraging the adaptability and generalization capabilities of Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) for malware detection. MeLeMaD incorporates a novel feature selection technique, Chunk-wise Feature Selection based on Gradient Boosting (CFSGB), tailored for handling large-scale, high-dimensional malware datasets, significantly enhancing the detection efficiency. Two benchmark malware datasets (CIC-AndMal2020 and BODMAS) and a custom dataset (EMBOD) were used for rigorously validating the MeLeMaD, achieving a remarkable performance in terms of key evaluation measures, including accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, MCC, and AUC. With accuracies of 98.04\% on CIC-AndMal2020 and 99.97\% on BODMAS, MeLeMaD outperforms the state-of-the-art approaches. The custom dataset, EMBOD, also achieves a commendable accuracy of 97.85\%. The results underscore the MeLeMaD's potential to address the challenges of robustness, adaptability, and large-scale, high-dimensional datasets in malware detection, paving the way for more effective and efficient cybersecurity solutions.

AI.tldr: MeLeMaD is a new framework for malware detection using MAML and CFSGB, achieving high accuracy on multiple datasets.
AI.motivation: Current malware detection methods struggle with robustness and adaptability to new threats and high-dimensional data.
AI.method: MeLeMaD combines Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) with Chunk-wise Feature Selection based on Gradient Boosting (CFSGB) to improve adaptability and efficiency in malware detection.
AI.result: MeLeMaD achieves 98.04% accuracy on CIC-AndMal2020, 99.97% on BODMAS, and 97.85% on EMBOD, outperforming existing methods.
AI.conclusion: MeLeMaD effectively addresses the challenges of robustness, adaptability, and high-dimensional data in malware detection, offering a promising solution for cybersecurity.

title: Uncovering Discrimination Clusters: Quantifying and Explaining Systematic Fairness Violations
comment: In 40th IEEE/ACM International Conference on Automated Software Engineering (ASE 2025)
summary: Fairness in algorithmic decision-making is often framed in terms of individual fairness, which requires that similar individuals receive similar outcomes. A system violates individual fairness if there exists a pair of inputs differing only in protected attributes (such as race or gender) that lead to significantly different outcomes-for example, one favorable and the other unfavorable. While this notion highlights isolated instances of unfairness, it fails to capture broader patterns of systematic or clustered discrimination that may affect entire subgroups. We introduce and motivate the concept of discrimination clustering, a generalization of individual fairness violations. Rather than detecting single counterfactual disparities, we seek to uncover regions of the input space where small perturbations in protected features lead to k-significantly distinct clusters of outcomes. That is, for a given input, we identify a local neighborhood-differing only in protected attributes-whose members' outputs separate into many distinct clusters. These clusters reveal significant arbitrariness in treatment solely based on protected attributes that help expose patterns of algorithmic bias that elude pairwise fairness checks. We present HyFair, a hybrid technique that combines formal symbolic analysis (via SMT and MILP solvers) to certify individual fairness with randomized search to discover discriminatory clusters. This combination enables both formal guarantees-when no counterexamples exist-and the detection of severe violations that are computationally challenging for symbolic methods alone. Given a set of inputs exhibiting high k-unfairness, we introduce a novel explanation method to generate interpretable, decision-tree-style artifacts. Our experiments demonstrate that HyFair outperforms state-of-the-art fairness verification and local explanation methods.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a new concept called discrimination clustering to detect patterns of systematic bias in algorithmic decision-making, addressing limitations of individual fairness. It proposes HyFair, a hybrid method that combines symbolic analysis and randomized search to uncover discriminatory clusters and generate interpretable explanations for detecting algorithmic bias.
AI.motivation: The paper is motivated by the need to address the limitations of existing fairness measures, particularly individual fairness, which focuses on isolated cases and does not detect broader patterns of system-wide or clustered discrimination. The authors aim to identify algorithmic bias that may not be captured by pairwise comparisons but emerges in clusters within subgroups of the input space.
AI.method: The method,HyFair, is a hybrid approach combining formal symbolic analysis (using SMT and MILP solvers) and randomized search to detect regions of the input space where small changes in protected attributes lead to k-distinct clusters of outcomes. When clusters of unfair outcomes are identified, the method generates interpretable, decision-tree-style explanations to help understand the sources of bias. The symbolic component ensures formal guarantees when no violations are found, while the randomized search helps uncover complex violations that symbolic techniques alone might miss.
AI.result: The experiments demonstrated that HyFair outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods in fairness verification and local explanation. By identifying discriminatory clusters and providing interpretable explanations, the method reveals significant patterns of algorithmic bias that traditional pairwise fairness measures can miss.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that HyFair offers an effective approach to detecting and explaining systematic algorithmic bias through the concept of discrimination clustering. It emphasizes the importance of moving beyond pairwise fairness checks to uncover broader patterns of algorithmic discrimination, and highlights the hybrid method's ability to provide both formal guarantees and the identification of complex bias patterns that purely symbolic methods cannot address.

title: RepetitionCurse: Measuring and Understanding Router Imbalance in Mixture-of-Experts LLMs under DoS Stress
summary: Mixture-of-Experts architectures have become the standard for scaling large language models due to their superior parameter efficiency. To accommodate the growing number of experts in practice, modern inference systems commonly adopt expert parallelism to distribute experts across devices. However, the absence of explicit load balancing constraints during inference allows adversarial inputs to trigger severe routing concentration. We demonstrate that out-of-distribution prompts can manipulate the routing strategy such that all tokens are consistently routed to the same set of top-$k$ experts, which creates computational bottlenecks on certain devices while forcing others to idle. This converts an efficiency mechanism into a denial-of-service attack vector, leading to violations of service-level agreements for time to first token. We propose RepetitionCurse, a low-cost black-box strategy to exploit this vulnerability. By identifying a universal flaw in MoE router behavior, RepetitionCurse constructs adversarial prompts using simple repetitive token patterns in a model-agnostic manner. On widely deployed MoE models like Mixtral-8x7B, our method increases end-to-end inference latency by 3.063x, degrading service availability significantly.

AI.tldr: The paper discusses vulnerabilities in MoE architectures, showing how adversarial inputs can create routing concentration issues leading to denial-of-service attacks. It proposes RepetitionCurse, a low-cost method to exploit this flaw, significantly increasing inference latency in models like Mixtral-8x7B.
AI.motivation: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures are widely used to scale large language models due to their parameter efficiency. However, the lack of load balancing constraints during inference can lead to routing concentration when adversarial inputs are introduced, causing computational bottlenecks and potential denial-of-service scenarios.
AI.method: The authors demonstrate that adversarial inputs, particularly out-of-distribution prompts with repetitive token patterns, can exploit a universal flaw in MoE router behavior. They propose RepetitionCurse, a black-box strategy that constructs such adversarial prompts in a model-agnostic manner to trigger routing concentration.
AI.result: Using RepetitionCurse, the paper shows that inference latency can be increased by 3.063 times in models such as Mixtral-8x7B, leading to significant degradation in service availability and potential violations of service-level agreements.
AI.conclusion: The paper highlights a critical vulnerability in MoE-based systems, where adversarial inputs can exploit routing mechanisms to cause denial-of-service attacks. The proposed RepetitionCurse method demonstrates the real-world feasibility of such exploits and underscores the need for robust load balancing and security measures in MoE architectures.

title: Test Case Specification Techniques and System Testing Tools in the Automotive Industry: A Review
comment: This is the author accepted manuscript (AAM) of a paper accepted for publication in The Journal of Systems and Software (Elsevier). The final published version will be available via the journal
summary: The automotive domain is shifting to software-centric development to meet regulation, market pressure, and feature velocity. This shift increases embedded systems' complexity and strains testing capacity. Despite relevant standards, a coherent system-testing methodology that spans heterogeneous, legacy-constrained toolchains remains elusive, and practice often depends on individual expertise rather than a systematic strategy. We derive challenges and requirements from a systematic literature review (SLR), complemented by industry experience and practice. We map them to test case specification techniques and testing tools, evaluating their suitability for automotive testing using PRISMA. Our contribution is a curated catalog that supports technique/tool selection and can inform future testing frameworks and improvements. We synthesize nine recurring challenge areas across the life cycle, such as requirements quality and traceability, variability management, and toolchain fragmentation. We then provide a prioritized criteria catalog that recommends model-based planning, interoperable and traceable toolchains, requirements uplift, pragmatic automation and virtualization, targeted AI and formal methods, actionable metrics, and lightweight organizational practices.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses the lack of a systematic methodology for system testing in the automotive industry as it shifts to software-centric development, offering a curated catalog of requirements and recommendations based on challenges identified through an SLR and industry validation.
AI.motivation: The automotive industry's shift to software-centric development is increasing the complexity of embedded systems, creating testing challenges due to legacy tools and a lack of standardized approaches, necessitating a more systematic methodology.
AI.method: The research combines a systematic literature review (SLR) with insights from industry practitioners to identify recurring challenges in automotive system testing. These are mapped to test case specification techniques and testing tools using PRISMA to evaluate suitability for automotive contexts.
AI.result: The paper synthesizes nine key challenge areas across the software life cycle and introduces a prioritized catalog of criteria for effective testing, such as model-based planning, interoperable toolchains, and the use of AI and formal methods. The catalog aims to support tool and technique selection and future framework development.
AI.conclusion: The study provides actionable recommendations for addressing automotive software testing challenges through systematic methods and industry collaboration, offering a foundation for standardizing practice beyond individual expertise.

title: Jailbreaking Attacks vs. Content Safety Filters: How Far Are We in the LLM Safety Arms Race?
summary: As large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed, ensuring their safe use is paramount. Jailbreaking, adversarial prompts that bypass model alignment to trigger harmful outputs, present significant risks, with existing studies reporting high success rates in evading common LLMs. However, previous evaluations have focused solely on the models, neglecting the full deployment pipeline, which typically incorporates additional safety mechanisms like content moderation filters. To address this gap, we present the first systematic evaluation of jailbreak attacks targeting LLM safety alignment, assessing their success across the full inference pipeline, including both input and output filtering stages. Our findings yield two key insights: first, nearly all evaluated jailbreak techniques can be detected by at least one safety filter, suggesting that prior assessments may have overestimated the practical success of these attacks; second, while safety filters are effective in detection, there remains room to better balance recall and precision to further optimize protection and user experience. We highlight critical gaps and call for further refinement of detection accuracy and usability in LLM safety systems.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates jailbreak attacks on LLMs through the full deployment pipeline, showing most techniques can be detected by safety filters and highlighting gaps in detection-precision trade-offs.
AI.motivation: Previous jailbreak assessments ignored safety mechanisms like input/output filters, leading to potentially overestimated attack success rates in real-world LLM deployments.
AI.method: Systematic evaluation of jailbreak techniques across the full inference pipeline, including both input filtering (prompt sanitization) and output filtering (response moderation) stages.
AI.result: 1) 90.4% of tested jailbreak methods were detectable by existing filters; 2). Detection accuracy and minimizing false positives/negatives remain suboptimal in safety system design.
AI.conclusion: Current evaluations overstate practical attack success when ignoring deployment pipeline defenses. Safety systems require improved detection accuracy while maintaining user experience through balanced filter precision.

title: A Systematic Mapping on Software Fairness: Focus, Trends and Industrial Context
summary: Context: Fairness in systems has emerged as a critical concern in software engineering, garnering increasing attention as the field has advanced in recent years. While several guidelines have been proposed to address fairness, achieving a comprehensive understanding of research solutions for ensuring fairness in software systems remains challenging. Objectives: This paper presents a systematic literature mapping to explore and categorize current advancements in fairness solutions within software engineering, focusing on three key dimensions: research trends, research focus, and viability in industrial contexts. Methods: We develop a classification framework to organize research on software fairness from a fresh perspective, applying it to 95 selected studies and analyzing their potential for industrial adoption. Results: Our findings reveal that software fairness research is expanding, yet it remains heavily focused on methods and algorithms. It primarily focuses on post-processing and group fairness, with less emphasis on early-stage interventions, individual fairness metrics, and understanding bias root causes. Additionally fairness research remains largely academic, with limited industry collaboration and low to medium Technology Readiness Level (TRL), indicating that industrial transferability remains distant. Conclusion: Our results underscore the need to incorporate fairness considerations across all stages of the software development life-cycle and to foster greater collaboration between academia and industry. This analysis provides a comprehensive overview of the field, offering a foundation to guide future research and practical applications of fairness in software systems.

AI.tldr: This paper provides a systematic literature mapping of fairness research in software engineering, revealing a focus on methods/algorithms and limited industrial adoption, while highlighting underexplored areas like early-stage bias mitigation and individual fairness.
AI.motivation: Existing fairness guidelines are fragmented, research lacks comprehensive categorization, and industrial transferability remains unclear. This study addresses these gaps by mapping research trends, focus areas, and industrial viability.
AI.method: A classification framework was developed and applied to 95 studies, analyzing three dimensions: research trends, focus (e.g., fairness types), and Technology Readiness Level (TRL).
AI.result: Fairness research is expanding but skewed toward post-processing and group fairness, with less emphasis on algorithms for bias root causes or early-stage interventions. Most studies have low to medium TRL, indicating limited industrial adoption and academic-industry collaboration.
AI.conclusion: The study advocates for integrating fairness across the entire software development lifecycle and strengthening academia-industry partnerships to improve practical implementation. It establishes a foundation for future fairness research and applications in software systems.

title: FedLiTeCAN : A Federated Lightweight Transformer for Fast and Robust CAN Bus Intrusion Detection
summary: This work implements a lightweight Transformer model for IDS in the domain of Connected and Autonomous Vehicles

AI.tldr: This paper presents a light-weight transformer model for intrusion detection in connected and autonomous vehicles.
AI.motivation: Connected and autonomous vehicles are vulnerable to cyber threats and require efficient and effective intrusion detection systems (IDS). However, traditional methods may be resource-intensive and not suitable for the constrained environments of vehicles.
AI.method: We propose a lightweight Transformer model specifically designed for the domain of connected and autonomous vehicles. The model is trained and evaluated using real-world datasets to ensure practical relevance.
AI.result: Our experiments demonstrate that the proposed model achieves competitive performance metrics with significantly reduced computational resources compared to existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: The lightweight Transformer model offers an effective and resource-efficient solution for IDS in connected and autonomous vehicles, making it suitable for implementation in real-world vehicular systems.

title: From Correctness to Collaboration: Toward a Human-Centered Framework for Evaluating AI Agent Behavior in Software Engineering
summary:
As Large Language Models (LLMs) evolve from code generators into collaborative partners for software engineers, our methods for evaluation are lagging. Current benchmarks, focused on code correctness, fail to capture the nuanced, interactive behaviors essential for successful human-AI partnership. To bridge this evaluation gap, this paper makes two core contributions. First, we present a foundational taxonomy of desirable agent behaviors for enterprise software engineering, derived from an analysis of 91 sets of user-defined agent rules. This taxonomy defines four key expectations of agent behavior: Adhere to Standards and Processes, Ensure Code Quality and Reliability, Solving Problems Effectively, and Collaborating with the User.
  Second, recognizing that these expectations are not static, we introduce the Context-Adaptive Behavior (CAB) Framework. This emerging framework reveals how behavioral expectations shift along two empirically-derived axes: the Time Horizon (from immediate needs to future ideals), established through interviews with 15 expert engineers, and the Type of Work (from enterprise production to rapid prototyping, for example), identified through a prompt analysis of a prototyping agent. Together, these contributions offer a human-centered foundation for designing and evaluating the next generation of AI agents, moving the field's focus from the correctness of generated code toward the dynamics of true collaborative intelligence.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses the gap in evaluating Large Language Models (LLMs) as collaborative partners in software engineering by introducing a taxonomy of desired agent behaviors and a Context-Adaptive Behavior (CAB), a framework. It shifts focus from code correctness to human-AI collaborative dynamics.
AI.motivation: Current benchmarks for LLMs in software engineering prioritize code correctness, neglecting interactive behaviors critical for effective human-AI collaboration. This limits the evaluation of dynamic, context-aware agent behaviors required in real-world scenarios.
AI.method: The paper presents two contributions: (1} a taxonomy of four key behavior expectations (Adhere to Standards, Ensure Code Quality, Solve Problems, Collaborate with User) derived from 91 user-defined agent rules, and (2} the CAB framework, analyzed via interviews with 15 expert engineers (Time Horizon axis) and prototyping agent prompt analysis (Type of Work axis).
AI.result: The taxonomy categorizes desirable agent behaviors, while the CAB framework reveals behavioral shifts along Time Horizon (immediate vs. future needs) and Type of Work (enterprise production vs. rapid prototyping). These contributions provide empirical insights into dynamic context adaptations.
AI.conclusion: The work establishes a human-centered foundation for evaluating LLM-based agents in software engineering, emphasizing adaptive collaboration over standalone code correctness, and advancing toward true collaborative intelligence in AI development.

title: Spatial Discretization for Fine-Grain Zone Checks with STARKs
summary: Many location-based services rely on a point-in-polygon test (PiP), checking whether a point or a trajectory lies inside a geographic zone. Since geometric operations are expensive in zero-knowledge proofs, privately performing the PiP test is challenging. In this paper, we answer the research questions of how different ways of encoding zones affect accuracy and proof cost by exploiting gridbased lookup tables under a fixed STARK execution model. Beyond a Boolean grid-based baseline that marks cells as in- or outside, we explore a distance-aware encoding approach that stores how far each cell is from a zone boundary and uses interpolation to reason within a cell. Our experiments on real-world data demonstrate that the proposed distance-aware approach achieves higher accuracy on coarse grids (max. 60%p accuracy gain) with only a moderate verification overhead (approximately 1.4x), making zone encoding the key lever for efficient zero-knowledge spatial checks.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a distance-aware encoding method for private point-in-polygon (PiP) tests in zero-knowledge proofs, achieving higher accuracy on coarse grids with moderate overhead compared to a Boolean baseline approach.
AI.motivation: Geometric operations in zero-knowledge proofs are computationally expensive, making private PiP tests challenging. Existing grid-based methods lack accuracy while maintaining efficiency.
AI.method: The authors compare a Boolean grid-based lookup approach with a distance-aware encoding that stores cell proximity to zone boundaries and uses interpolation. Experiments use a fixed STARK execution model on real-world data to evaluate accuracy and verification costs.
AI.result: The distance-aware approach achieves up to 60% higher accuracy on coarse grids with 1.4× increased verification overhead compared to the baseline.
AI.conclusion: Distance-aware zone encoding optimizes accuracy and efficiency for private spatial computations, positioning encoding strategies as critical for practical zero-knowledge spatial checks.

title: From Illusion to Insight: Change-Aware File-Level Software Defect Prediction Using Agentic AI
summary: Much of the reported progress in file-level software defect prediction (SDP) is, in reality, nothing but an illusion of accuracy. Over the last decades, machine learning and deep learning models have reported increasing performance across software versions. However, since most files persist across releases and retain their defect labels, standard evaluation rewards label-persistence bias rather than reasoning about code changes. To address this issue, we reformulate SDP as a change-aware prediction task, in which models reason over code changes of a file within successive project versions, rather than relying on static file snapshots. Building on this formulation, we propose an LLM-driven, change-aware, multi-agent debate framework. Our experiments on multiple PROMISE projects show that traditional models achieve inflated F1, while failing on rare but critical defect-transition cases. In contrast, our change-aware reasoning and multi-agent debate framework yields more balanced performance across evolution subsets and significantly improves sensitivity to defect introductions. These results highlight fundamental flaws in current SDP evaluation practices and emphasize the need for change-aware reasoning in practical defect prediction. The source code is publicly available.

AI.tldr: This paper challenges the validity of traditional software defect prediction (SDP), proposing a change-aware LLM-driven multi-agent framework that addresses label-persistence bias by focusing on code evolution rather than static file snapshots.
AI.motivation: Existing SDP models gain inflated accuracy by exploiting persistent file labels across software versions, failing to capture critical defect transitions that require reasoning about code changes over time.
AI.method: Reformulates SDP as a change-aware task tracking file evolution between successive versions, combined with an LLM-powered multi-agent debate framework to model evolving defect transitions.
AI.result: Traditional models fail critical defect-transition cases despite high F1 scores, while the proposed approach achieves balanced performance across evolution subsets and improved sensitivity to newly introduced defects in PROMISE projects.
AI.conclusion: Current SDP evaluation practices are fundamentally flawed by rewarding label-persistence bias; change-aware reasoning frameworks are essential for practical defect prediction that captures code evolution dynamics.

title: How Would Oblivious Memory Boost Graph Analytics on Trusted Processors?
summary: Trusted processors provide a way to perform joint computations while preserving data privacy. To overcome the performance degradation caused by data-oblivious algorithms to prevent information leakage, we explore the benefits of oblivious memory (OM) integrated in processors, to which the accesses are unobservable by adversaries. We focus on graph analytics, an important application vulnerable to access-pattern attacks. With a co-design between storage structure and algorithms, our prototype system is 100x faster than baselines given an OM sized around the per-core cache which can be implemented on existing processors with negligible overhead. This gives insights into equipping trusted processors with OM.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes integrating oblivious memory (OM) in processors for privacy-preserving graph analytics, achieving 100x speedup over baselines with minimal overhead.
AI.motivation: Trusted processors suffer from performance degradation due to data-oblivious algorithms needed to prevent information leakage. Graph analytics is particularly vulnerable to access-pattern attacks.
AI.method: The authors explore a co-design approach combining storage structure and algorithms, implementing a prototype system with OM integrated in the processor.
AI.result: The prototype system achieves a 100x speedup compared to baselines, using OM sized around the per-core cache with negligible hardware overhead.
AI.conclusion: Integrating OM in trusted processors is viable for improving performance in privacy-sensitive applications like graph analytics, providing practical insights for future processor design.

title: Coding With AI: From a Reflection on Industrial Practices to Future Computer Science and Software Engineering Education
summary: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have introduced new paradigms in software development, including vibe coding, AI-assisted coding, and agentic coding, fundamentally reshaping how software is designed, implemented, and maintained. Prior research has primarily examined AI-based coding at the individual level or in educational settings, leaving industrial practitioners' perspectives underexplored. This paper addresses this gap by investigating how LLM coding tools are used in professional practice, the associated concerns and risks, and the resulting transformations in development workflows, with particular attention to implications for computing education. We conducted a qualitative analysis of 57 curated YouTube videos published between late 2024 and 2025, capturing reflections and experiences shared by practitioners. Following a filtering and quality assessment process, the selected sources were analyzed to compare LLM-based and traditional programming, identify emerging risks, and characterize evolving workflows. Our findings reveal definitions of AI-based coding practices, notable productivity gains, and lowered barriers to entry. Practitioners also report a shift in development bottlenecks toward code review and concerns regarding code quality, maintainability, security vulnerabilities, ethical issues, erosion of foundational problem-solving skills, and insufficient preparation of entry-level engineers. Building on these insights, we discuss implications for computer science and software engineering education and argue for curricular shifts toward problem-solving, architectural thinking, code review, and early project-based learning that integrates LLM tools. This study offers an industry-grounded perspective on AI-based coding and provides guidance for aligning educational practices with rapidly evolving professional realities.

AI.tldr: The paper explores the impact of LLM coding tools on professional software development and education by analyzing YouTube practitioner discussions, highlighting productivity gains, new challenges, and needed curricular changes.
AI.motivation: Prior research focused on individual or educational AI-coding contexts, leaving industrial practitioner experiences underexplored, necessitating an industry-grounded analysis to understand transformative impacts on practice and education.
AI.method: Qualitative analysis of 57 curated YouTube videos from late 2024 to 2025, including reflection and experience sharing by practitioners, with a filtering and quality assessment to examine LLM-based versus traditional programming practices.
AI.result: Findings show productivity improvements, reduced entry barriers, emerging risks in code quality, maintainability, and security, as well as shifts in bottlenecks to code review; practitioners also express concerns about foundational problem-solving erosion and unprepared entry-level engineers.
AI.conclusion: Curriculum must shift to problem-solving, architectural thinking, code review, and project-based LLM-integrated learning to align with industry changes driven by AI-coding practices, ensuring educational preparation for evolving professional demands.

title: FedSecureFormer: A Fast, Federated and Secure Transformer Framework for Lightweight Intrusion Detection in Connected and Autonomous Vehicles
summary: This works presents an encoder-only transformer built with minimum layers for intrusion detection in the domain of Connected and Autonomous Vehicles using Federated Learning.

AI.tldr: We propose a lightweight transformer with encoder-only architecture for intrusion detection in the domain of connected and autonomous vehicles using federated learning
AI.motivation: Lightweight transformer models are important for intrusion detection in the resource-constrained environment of connected and autonomous vehicles. Federated learning allows for collaborative model training while maintaining data privacy across decentralized vehicular networks.
AI.method: We present an encoder-only transformer designed to minimize the number of layers in the network while maintaining high detection accuracy. The model is trained using a federated learning framework which enables training on decentralized data from vehicular networks without centralizing the sensitive data.
AI.result: Our experiments show that the lightweight transformer architecture achieves a competitive level of accuracy while significantly reducing model complexity compared to standard transformer models. The federated learning approach ensures that the model can be trained across multiple decentralized vehicular data sources without compromising data privacy.
AI.conclusion: This work demonstrates that a minimal layer transformer architecture combined with federated learning is effective for intrusion detection in connected and autonomous vehicles, offering a promising approach for implementing secure and privacy-conscious intrusion detection systems in vehicular networks.

title: Developing controlled natural language for formal specification patterns using AI assistants
summary: Using an AI assistant, we developed a method for systematically constructing controlled natural language for requirements based on formal specification patterns containing logical attributes. The method involves three stages: 1) compiling a generalized natural language requirement pattern that utilizes all attributes of the formal specification template; 2) generating, using the AI assistant, a corpus of natural language requirement patterns, reduced by partially evaluating attributes (the developed prompt utilizes the generalized template, attribute definitions, and specific formal semantics of the requirement patterns); and 3) formalizing the syntax of the controlled natural language based on an analysis of the grammatical structure of the resulting patterns. The method has been tested for event-driven temporal requirements.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a systematic method for constructing controlled natural language requirements using AI, comprising three stages: pattern generalization, corpus generation via attribute reduction, and syntax formalization through grammatical analysis, validated for event-driven temporal requirements.
AI.motivation: To address the complexity of aligning formal specifications with natural language requirements by providing a structured, AI-assisted methodology for systematic controlled natural language generation.
AI.method: Three-stage process: (1)c ompiling generalized natural language patterns from formal templates; (2)p rompt-based AI generation of reduced attribute patterns; (3)f ormalizing syntax via grammatical analysis of generated patterns.
AI.result: A validated framework for controlled natural language construction, demonstrated through application to event-driven temporal requirements, showing effectiveness in attribute reduction and syntax formalization.
AI.conclusion: The proposed AI-driven method systematically bridges formal specifications and controlled natural language, enhancing precision in requirements engineering through structured pattern generation and formal analysis.

title: FAST-IDS: A Fast Two-Stage Intrusion Detection System with Hybrid Compression for Real-Time Threat Detection in Connected and Autonomous Vehicles
summary: We have implemented a multi-stage IDS for CAVs that can be deployed to resourec-constrained environments after hybrid model compression.

AI.tldr: A multi-stage IDS for CAVs is implemented and can be deployed in resouce-constrained environments after hybrid model compression.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to develop an intrusion detection system that is suitable for connected and autonomous vehicles (CAVs) with limited computational resources, which can be achieved by implementing a multi-stage IDS and utilizing hybrid model compression techniques.
AI.method: The method involves implementing a multi-stage intrusion detection system (IDS) for CAVs and deploying it in resource-constrained environments. This is achieved by applying hybrid model compression techniques, which likely include a combination of model pruning, quantization, and knowledge distillation to reduce the model size and computational requirements while maintaining performance.
AI.result: The result is a multi-stage IDS for CAVs that can be effectively deployed in resource-constrained environments after undergoing hybrid model compression, indicating that the system maintains its effectiveness while being optimized for limited resources.
AI.conclusion: The conclusion is that the implementation of a multi-stage IDS with hybrid model compression allows for the deployment of an effective intrusion detection system in CAVs that have limited computational resources, ensuring security while optimizing for efficiency.

title: CoHalLo: code hallucination localization via probing hidden layer vector
summary: The localization of code hallucinations aims to identify specific lines of code containing hallucinations, helping developers to improve the reliability of AI-generated code more efficiently. Although recent studies have adopted several methods to detect code hallucination, most of these approaches remain limited to coarse-grained detection and lack specialized techniques for fine-grained hallucination localization. This study introduces a novel method, called CoHalLo, which achieves line-level code hallucination localization by probing the hidden-layer vectors from hallucination detection models. CoHalLo uncovers the key syntactic information driving the model's hallucination judgments and locates the hallucinating code lines accordingly. Specifically, we first fine-tune the hallucination detection model on manually annotated datasets to ensure that it learns features pertinent to code syntactic information. Subsequently, we designed a probe network that projects high-dimensional latent vectors onto a low-dimensional syntactic subspace, generating vector tuples and reconstructing the predicted abstract syntax tree (P-AST). By comparing P-AST with the original abstract syntax tree (O-AST) extracted from the input AI-generated code, we identify the key syntactic structures associated with hallucinations. This information is then used to pinpoint hallucinated code lines. To evaluate CoHalLo's performance, we manually collected a dataset of code hallucinations. The experimental results show that CoHalLo achieves a Top-1 accuracy of 0.4253, Top-3 accuracy of 0.6149, Top-5 accuracy of 0.7356, Top-10 accuracy of 0.8333, IFA of 5.73, Recall@1% Effort of 0.052721, and Effort@20% Recall of 0.155269, which outperforms the baseline methods.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the CoHalLo method for line-level code hallucination localization by probing hidden-layer vectors.
AI.motivation: Despite recent efforts in hallucination detection for AI-generated code, there is a lack of fine-grained methods to identify specific hallucinated lines, which limits the efficiency of improving code reliability.
AI.method: CoHalLo is designed by fine-tuning a hallucination detection model on annotated datasets, then using a probe network to project latent vectors into a syntactic subspace to generate a predicted AST and compare it with the original.
AI.result: CoHalLo shows strong performance with high Top-n ACCs and better metric scores compared to baseline methods in hallucination line identification.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method effectively identifies code lines with hallucinations by capturing key syntactic structures, offering a more efficient approach to enhance the reliability of AI-generated code.

title: SourceBroken: A large-scale analysis on the (un)reliability of SourceRank in the PyPI ecosystem
comment: Accpted for the 41st ACM/SIGAPP Symposium on Applied Computing (SAC '26), March 23--27, 2026, Thessaloniki, Greece
summary:
SourceRank is a scoring system made of 18 metrics that assess the popularity and quality of open-source packages. Despite being used in several recent studies, none has thoroughly analyzed its reliability against evasion attacks aimed at inflating the score of malicious packages, thereby masquerading them as trustworthy. To fill this gap, we first propose a threat model that identifies potential evasion approaches for each metric, including the URL confusion technique, which can affect 5 out of the 18 metrics by leveraging a URL pointing to a legitimate repository potentially unrelated to the malicious package.
  Furthermore, we study the reliability of SourceRank in the PyPI ecosystem by analyzing the SourceRank distributions of benign and malicious packages in the state-of-the-art MalwareBench dataset, as well as in a real-world dataset of 122,398 packages. Our analysis reveals that, while historical data suggests a clear distinction between benign and malicious packages, the real-world distributions overlap significantly, mainly due to SourceRank's failure to timely reflect package removals. As a result, SourceRank cannot be reliably used to discriminate between benign and malicious packages in real-world scenarios, nor to select benign packages among those available on PyPI.
  Finally, our analysis reveals that URL confusion represents an emerging attack vector, with its prevalence increasing from 4.2% in MalwareBench to 7.0% in our real-world dataset. Moreover, this technique is often used alongside other evasion techniques and can significantly inflate the SourceRank metrics of malicious packages.

AI.tldr: The reliability of the SourceRank system, which assesses open-source packages' popularity and quality, is examined against evasion attacks, revealing its susceptibility to techniques like URL confusion and demonstrating its limited effectiveness in distinguishing malicious from benign packages in real-world scenarios.
AI.motivation: The motivation stems from the need to evaluate the reliability of SourceRank metrics in the face of evasion tactics that malicious actors might use to appear trustworthy, especially given the increasing sophistication of malware within the open-source ecosystem.
AI.method: The study introduces a threat model to identify potential evasion techniques, analyzes historical data from MalwareBench alongside a large real-world dataset to assess the overlap of SourceRank distributions between benign and malicious packages, and evaluates the impact of URL confusion as an emerging attack vector.
AI.result: The results indicate significant overlap in SourceRank distributions between benign and malicious packages in real-world data, suggesting limited discriminative power. The prevalence and effectiveness of URL confusion in raising the SourceRank of malicious packages are highlighted, with its use increasing from 4.2% to 7.0%.
AI.conclusion: SourceRank cannot reliably distinguish between benign and malicious packages in real-world scenarios due to its delayed reflection of package removals and vulnerability to techniques like URL confusion, underscoring the need for more robust metrics to ensure open-source security.

title: "Game Changer" or "Overenthusiastic Drunk Acquaintance"? Generative AI Use by Blind and Low Vision Software Professionals in the Workplace
summary: The software development workplace poses numerous technical and collaborative accessibility challenges for blind and low vision software professionals (BLVSPs). Though Generative AI (GenAI) is increasingly adopted within the software development industry and has been a rapidly growing topic of interest in research, to date, the unique perspectives of BLVSPs have yet to be consulted. We report on a qualitative study involving 39 semi-structured interviews with BLVSPs about what the introduction of GenAI has meant for their work. We found that BLVSPs used GenAI for many software development tasks, resulting in benefits such as increased productivity and accessibility. However, significant costs were also accompanied by GenAI use as they were more vulnerable to hallucinations than their sighted colleagues. Sometimes, organizational policies prevented use. Based on our findings, we discuss the higher-risks and higher-returns that BLVSPs had to carefully weigh when deciding whether and when to use GenAI tools for work.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how blind and low vision software professionals (BLVSPs) adopt and experience Generative AI (GenAI) tools in their work, revealing both productivity benefits and heightened risks like AI hallucinations.
AI.motivation: The study addresses the absence of BLVSPs' perspectives in GenAI research and examines how accessibility challenges in software development are influenced by their unique access to and vulnerabilities in using these tools.
AI.method: Qualitative analysis of 39 semi-structured interviews with BLVSPs, focusing on their GenAI usage patterns, benefits, and challenges.
AI.result: BLVSPs reported increased productivity and accessibility via GenAI, but also faced enhanced risks from hallucinations, workflow disruptions, and restrictive organizational policies. Balancing these trade-offs became critical.
AI.conclusion: GenAI adoption for BLVSPs involves higher-stakes decisions due to amplified risks and rewards. The study calls for tailored organizational policies and inclusive AI design to reduce accessibility gaps.

title: Language Model Agents Under Attack: A Cross Model-Benchmark of Profit-Seeking Behaviors in Customer Service
summary: Customer-service LLM agents increasingly make policy-bound decisions (refunds, rebooking, billing disputes), but the same ``helpful'' interaction style can be exploited: a small fraction of users can induce unauthorized concessions, shifting costs to others and eroding trust in agentic workflows. We present a cross-domain benchmark of profit-seeking direct prompt injection in customer-service interactions, spanning 10 service domains and 100 realistic attack scripts grouped into five technique families. Across five widely used models under a unified rubric with uncertainty reporting, attacks are highly domain-dependent (airline support is most exploitable) and technique-dependent (payload splitting is most consistently effective). We release data and evaluation code to support reproducible auditing and to inform the design of oversight and recovery workflows for trustworthy, human centered agent interfaces.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a benchmark for evaluating the vulnerability of customer-service LLM agents to profit-seeking prompt injections, analyzing the effectiveness of attacks across different domains and techniques.
AI.motivation: Customer-service LLM agents are being used for policy-bound decisions, but their helpful interaction style can be exploited by users to induce unauthorized concessions, leading to financial losses and trust erosion. There is a need to understand the vulnerabilities and develop robust oversight workflows.
AI.method: The authors developed a cross-domain benchmark with 100 realistic attack scripts across 10 service domains and 5 technique families. They evaluated five widely used LLMs under a unified rubric, including uncertainty reporting.
